comments.body: There are various questions on this website regarding JavaFX and Google Maps (those exist, though your initial search did not reveal them).  Prior experience showed that the combination used to work with some older JavaFX versions, but did not work with newer versions.  I haven't tried the combination recently, so cannot comment on the current status of the interworking of the two technologies.

Some progress... The Google Maps release notes for v3.57.2 include the following update "Map raster tiles are now served in WebP image format". If I modify my v.3.57 test to load "roadmap" tiles, it fails with the "...no imagery here" message, but "satellite" tiles work. So, perhaps in one of the v3.58.x versions this format change has now been applied to satellite tiles. However, this isn't mentioned in the v3.85.x release notes. As far as I know, WebView doesn't support WebP. As for the Windows 10 vs 11 aspect, I still puzzled as satellite tiles load in all v3 releases.

"As far as I know, WebView doesn't support WebP." -> I don't know either, but you could ask a separate question about that and somebody might answer it. JavaFX 24 implemented JDK-8306707 Support pluggable image loading via javax.imageio. So if the WebView implementation in JavaFX supported that and it didn't already support WebP, maybe WebP support could be added via the new pluggable API. Again I don't know if that would be the case or even necessary for WebP support in WebView, nor if there would be additional issues with google maps.

So I tried adding an ImageIO WebP plugin (I used twelvemonkeys). I tested it by successfully displaying an image in a JavaFX Pane, but unfortunately it didn't help with WebView. As you suggested, I try asking a new question about WebP support in WebView.

answers.body:
I've successfully submitted a bug report. If you are interested and want to follow its progress you can track it using JDK-8353342.

It's been assigned priority P4, so may take a while to get fixed.

A quick update for anyone that is interested.

  1. The failure to display a Google Maps satellite image is actually related to the Windows display scale setting (and probably on Linux, although I've not yet tested this). It turns out that for the above code sample to work on Windows 11, the setting needs to be 100%. Interestingly, on Windows 10, when the setting is not 100%, the satellite image is displayed, but is incorrectly scaled.

  2. On both the Windows 10 and 11 platforms (and probably Linux) the other Google Map types (Road Map, Terrain, etc.) fail to display. As already mentioned above, I still believe that this is caused by the current lack of WEBP support in the JavaFX WebView class (satellite imagery is still being delivered using PNG tiles).

I'm in the process of updating my JavaFX bug report.

I hope the above is helpful.

title: JavaFX 21.0.6 WebView problem loading Google Maps v3.58+ on Windows 11
body:
Included below is a minimal application that attempts to load Google Maps using a JavaFX 21.0.6 application (compiled using the 21.0.5 JDK).

On a Windows 10 machine the code works as expected, but it fails to display the map when running under Windows 11 and instead displays a "Sorry, we have no imagery here" message.

The Java application code is show below:

import javafx.application.Application;
import javafx.application.Platform;
import javafx.scene.Scene;
import javafx.scene.web.WebView;
import javafx.stage.Stage;

import netscape.javascript.JSObject;

public class GoogleMaps extends Application
{
    public static final void main(final String[] args)
    {
        Application.launch(args);
    }

    @Override
    public final void start(final Stage stage) throws Exception
    {
        var webView = new WebView();
        var webEngine = webView.getEngine();
        webEngine.setJavaScriptEnabled(true);
        webEngine.setOnAlert(event -> {
            if ("inject:bridge".equals(event.getData()))
            {
                final var jsWindow = (JSObject)webEngine.executeScript("window");
                jsWindow.setMember("javabridge", GoogleMaps.this);
                webEngine.executeScript("console.log = (message) => javabridge.logConsole(message);");
            }
        });

        Platform.runLater(() -> webEngine.load(ClassLoader.getSystemClassLoader().getResource("maps.html").toString()));
        stage.setScene(new Scene(webView));
        stage.show();
    }

    public final void logConsole(final String message)
    {
        System.out.println("WebView: " + message);
    }
}

The associated maps.html is shown below:

<html>
    <head>
        <title>Google Maps</title>
        <style>
            body {margin: 0; padding: 0;}
            #canvas {width: 100%; height: 100%;}
        </style>
        <script>
            alert("inject:bridge");
        </script>
    </head>
    <body>
        <div id='canvas'></div>
        <script>
            window.addEventListener('error', event => {
                const {message, filename, lineno, colno, error} = event; 
                console.log('[Error] ' + message);
            });

            async function initMap()
            {
                console.log("Console logging is active");
                const map = new google.maps.Map(document.getElementById('canvas'), {zoom: 5, center: {lat: 52.2, lng: -1.3}, mapTypeId: 'satellite'});
            }
        </script>

        <!-- notes 1, version 3.58 or greater fails to display the map and shows the message 'Sorry, we have no imagery here', v3.57 works -->
        <!--       2, the API key has been removed, add '?key=xxx' with an ampersand (although, it should work in 'development mode' without a key) -->
        <!-- -->
        <script async defer src="https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/js?v=3.58&callback=initMap&loading=async"></script>
    </body>
</html>

Finally, in case anyone wants to build the application, I've included my Apache ant script:

<project name="Google Maps" default="jar" basedir=".">
    <description>Google Maps Test</description>

    <property environment="env"/>
    <property name="src" location="src"/>
    <property name="build" location="build"/>
    <property name="dist" location="."/>
    <property name="jarname" value="googlemaps.jar"/>

    <target name="init">
        <tstamp/>
        <mkdir dir="${build}"/>
    </target>

    <target name="tidy" description="Clean up, but leave the JAR file">
        <delete dir="${build}"/>
    </target>

    <target name="clean" description="Clean up">
        <delete dir="${build}"/>
        <delete file="${dist}/${jarname}"/>
    </target>

    <target name="compile" depends="clean,init" description="Compile source files">
        <echo>JavaFX Library Path: ${env.PATH_TO_JAVAFX}</echo>
        <javac includeantruntime="false" srcdir="${src}" destdir="${build}" modulepath="${env.PATH_TO_JAVAFX}">
            <compilerarg line="--add-modules ALL-MODULE-PATH"/>
            <compilerarg value="-version"/>
        </javac>
    </target>

    <target name="jar" depends="compile" description="Build the JAR file">
        <jar jarfile="${dist}/${jarname}">
            <fileset dir="${build}"/>
            <file name="maps.html"/>

            <manifest>
                <attribute name="Main-Class" value="GoogleMaps"/>
                <attribute name="Multi-Release" value="true"/>
            </manifest>
        </jar>
    </target>

    <target name="run" depends="jar" description="Build execute the JAR">
        <java jar="${dist}/${jarname}" modulepath="${env.PATH_TO_JAVAFX}" fork="true">
            <jvmarg line="--add-modules ALL-MODULE-PATH"/>
        </java>
    </target>
</project>

As far as I can tell no errors are being reported via console.log() or via the DOM window event handler. I'm hoping that done something silly, or perhaps someone else knows the answer or can suggest some things to try. I have already spent quite a bit of time experimenting, but with no success. This included building with JavaFX 24 (the latest release) and testing on other Windows 11 machines.

Perhaps this is an issue with the associated version of WebKit running under Windows 11? Although, this seems unlikely as I imagine by now it would have been encountered and reported by others (I did attempt to look for related posts and bug reports).

Any help or advice would be greatly appreciated!
